content.full_text:
California Fair Political Practices Commission

April 17, 1992

Earl E. Sullaway  
Deputy Director  
Enforcement Division  
Department of Fair Employment & Housing  
2014 T Street, Suite 210  
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance  
Our File No. I-92-74

Dear Mr. Sullaway:

I am writing to confirm our telephone conversation of March 6, 1992, wherein we discussed your request for advice No. I-92-74. The following is a summary of our discussion:

1. You are the Deputy Director for Enforcement of the Department of Fair Employment and Housing (DFEH). You would like to know if providing certain documents and products developed by DFEH to Mr. Calvin McGee, a former state official, would constitute a violation of Government Code Sections 87401 and 87406(d).

As we discussed, the revolving door provisions found in Government Code Sections 87401 and 87406(d) apply only to former state officials and employees. Since you are currently a state official, these provisions do not apply to you.

2. You next inquiry was whether Mr. McGee would be entitled to possession of the documents and products and whether such possession would constitute a violation for Mr. McGee.

I explained that we do not give third-party advice regarding whether another state official is in compliance with the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). Mr. McGee would have to contact us directly to discuss his duties and obligations under the Act.

3. You also expressed concerns about Government Code Section 83116.5. Specifically, if Mr. McGee is in violation of the revolving door provisions (Sections 87401 and 87406(d)), would you, by providing certain documents, be liable for "aiding and abetting" another person in violation of the Act.

As we discussed, this section has been examined by the FPPC and a policy determination has been made regarding attorneys who have filing and reporting obligations under the Act and who render advice concerning application of the Act. (See the Eckis Advice Letter, No. I-91-537, enclosed.) I realize that this interpretation does not directly address your question, but the policy consideration focuses on whether the conduct is grossly negligent.

4. Lastly, we discussed whether your request is the appropriate subject for an opinion under Regulation 18320. I explained to you that your questions can be handled at the staff level. However, if you desire to pursue a Commission opinion, your request will be forwarded to our Executive Director, who makes the determination as to whether a request is granted or denied.

I hope this summary has addressed your concerns. Please let me know how you would like to proceed at this point.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Jill Stecher
Counsel, Legal Division
